1: The word of YHWH comes again with a question: what is the vine tree compared to any other tree? This seemingly rhetorical opening sets up a Christological reversal. Israel had long been described as YHWH's vine (Psalms 80:8-11; Isaiah 5:1-7; Hosea 10:1), but Ezekiel 15 presses the vine metaphor to its breaking point. The Christological trajectory does not lead away from the vine image but through its failure to its fulfillment: Jesus declares himself "the true vine" (John 15:1 — h ampelos h alēthinē), the modifier "true" presupposing that Israel was the vine that proved false.
2: The divine question — what advantage does vine wood have over any branch from the forest trees? — anticipates the answer: none. The vine is valued only for its fruit; without fruit, its wood is worthless. The logic of the vine metaphor that runs through the OT (Ps 80; Isa 5; Jeremiah 2:21; Hosea 10:1) reaches its nadir here: a fruitless vine is not just a failed vine but a useless vine. Christ's teaching in John 15:1-6 operates within exactly this logic: the branch that does not bear fruit is taken away and thrown into the fire and burned — the same end as the vine wood of Ezekiel 15:4-6.
3: Can wood from the vine be used for any work? Can you make a peg from it to hang anything? The answer is no — vine wood is not useful for construction or even for the simplest utilitarian function (a peg). This contrasts sharply with the cedar (Ezekiel 17:3, 22), which provides building timber, or the oak and terebinth of the forest. The vine's only purpose is fruit; without fruitfulness, it has no other function. Jesus applies this logic directly: "I am the vine; you are the branches. Whoever abides in me and I in him, he it is that bears much fruit, for apart from me you can do nothing" (John 15:5). The "nothing" echoes the uselessness of vine wood.
4: Given to the fire for fuel — both ends are burned, and the middle is charred: is it then useful for any work? The escalation from useless to burned to charred makes it even more useless. The fire in view is YHWH's judgment (Ezekiel 15:7: "I will set my face against them"). In the Christological reading, this fire of judgment fell on Jerusalem in 586 BCE but anticipates the eschatological judgment from which only those who abide in the True Vine are preserved. John 15:6: "If anyone does not abide in me, he is thrown away like a branch and withers; and the branches are gathered, thrown into the fire, and burned."
5: If the vine was not useful for work when whole, how much less when fire has consumed it? The destroyed vine is even more worthless than the intact vine. The argument from the lesser (whole but useless) to the greater (burned and useless) reflects standard ancient near-eastern rhetorical logic. Christologically, the failed vine of Israel reaches its absolute zero here — the burned vine, representing the post-586 BCE remnant, becomes the void that Christ's True Vine fills. The argument presupposes that Israel was always the wrong kind of vine; Christ is the vine Israel could never become by itself (cf. John 15:4: "the branch cannot bear fruit by itself").
6: The allegorical application announced: "Therefore, thus says the Lord YHWH: like the wood of the vine among the trees of the forest, which I have given to the fire for fuel, so I will give up the inhabitants of Jerusalem." The vine-wood-to-fire allegory is applied to Jerusalem explicitly. The Christological significance lies in the contrast: where Ezekiel announces the giving of the inhabitants to fire, John 3:16 announces the giving of God's Son so that those who believe in him might not perish. The Son given is the inverse of the judgment giving — sacrificial giving that reverses the fire.
7: Face set against the inhabitants: "I will set my face against them; they have gone out from the fire, yet the fire shall consume them." The escaped survivors of prior judgments still face the final fire. Christologically, this absolute divine opposition cannot be escaped through human resourcefulness — only through the mediation of Christ who "bore our sins in his body on the tree" (1 Peter 2:24). The "tree" of the cross in 1 Peter 2:24 uses the same word (xylon) as the LXX vine/wood — Christ became the wood given to the fire so that those who trust him might not be consumed.
8: The land made desolate because of faithlessness: "And I will make the land desolate, because they have acted faithlessly, declares the Lord YHWH." The chapter closes with covenant language — ma'al (faithlessness/trespass) names the breach that brings desolation. The Christological resolution lies beyond this chapter: the desolated land of the exile becomes, through Christ, the renewed earth of the new creation. The True Vine that Israel failed to be is planted in the desolated ground and produces fruit that restores what the faithless vine destroyed (cf. Isaiah 27:2-6: a new vineyard song reversing Isaiah 5:1-7).
